audience.description: Developers who already implement foundational structures and now need proof-driven graph, optimization, randomized, approximation, and feasibility techniques.
entryKnowledge: Implement and analyze foundational sorting, searching, trees, heaps, hash tables, and graph traversals.
State invariants, build adversarial cases, and benchmark geometric input sizes.
deviceConstraints: All solvers use bounded local browser-worker instances and deterministic seeds.
accessibilityAssumptions: Proofs, graphs, tables, and residual networks have structured text forms.
No task depends on visual-only geometry, color, audio, or time pressure.

placementEvidence: Placement evidence must show this entry skill in working code: Implement and analyze foundational sorting, searching, trees, heaps, hash tables, and graph traversals.
Placement evidence must show this entry skill in working code: State invariants, build adversarial cases, and benchmark geometric input sizes.
completionEvidence: Complete and defend all 4 changed-case software projects with tests, trace evidence, and stakeholder acceptance.
Pass the cumulative performance examination at 85 percent or higher without solution-shape hints.

includes: proof techniques, amortized analysis, balanced trees, and disjoint sets
strong components, shortest paths, spanning trees, greedy design, and dynamic programming
backtracking, string algorithms, network flow, matching, randomization, and approximation
complexity boundaries, feasibility communication, resource budgets, and advanced review defense
excludes: foundational structure implementation already assessed in the prerequisite
research-level algorithm theory without an applied implementation or decision artifact

sources.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures
sources.scope: Canonical algorithm, complexity, abstract data type, graph, tree, sorting, and search terminology.

sources.title: MIT 6.046J Design and Analysis of Algorithms
sources.scope: Advanced analysis, divide and conquer, randomization, optimization, approximation, and graph algorithms.

sources.title: Python 3.14 Heap Queue Algorithms
sources.scope: Heap invariants, priority queues, merging, selection, and max-heap operations.

sources.title: Python 3.14 Graphlib Topological Ordering
sources.scope: Directed acyclic graph preparation, topological ordering, cycle detection, and parallel readiness.

projects.title: Predictable Dynamic Index
projects.stakeholder: A data platform needs ordered queries and changing connectivity with bounded long-run cost
projects.userNeed: Combine balanced indexing and disjoint-set operations with formal and measured amortized evidence.
constraints: Every cached field has an update invariant.
Amortized claims distinguish sequence cost from worst-case latency.

projects.title: Weighted Network Planning Board
projects.stakeholder: A regional planner must compare route, connection, and dependency decisions
projects.userNeed: Deliver strong-component, shortest-path, and minimum-spanning analyses with precondition and limitation evidence.
constraints: Algorithm choice follows weight and direction conditions.
Paths, cycles, and cuts are concrete artifacts.
Minimum cost is not misreported as resilience.

projects.title: Constraint and Allocation Optimizer
projects.stakeholder: A staffing group needs feasible assignments and transparent tradeoffs
projects.userNeed: Compare greedy, dynamic-programming, search, and flow models on changed constraints.
constraints: State and pruning rules are explicit.
Exact-model limits trigger a documented alternative.

projects.title: Bounded Planning Engine
projects.stakeholder: A technical review board needs a safe optimizer under uncertain scale
projects.userNeed: Ship exact, approximate, or randomized behavior with honest guarantees, budgets, and failure operations.
constraints: Guarantees and empirical outcomes are reported separately.
Timeout and partial-result semantics preserve truth.
Baseline, proof, tests, and resource evidence agree.
